title: The Nervous System and Mindful Resting: The Art of Doing Nothing as Healing
excerpt: Discover how mindful resting activates your nervous system’s healing power and why doing nothing can be the most effective form of self-care.
body:
Understanding the Nervous System’s Role in Healing

When you’re feeling stuck in your healing journey, it’s often because your nervous system remains dysregulated. Trauma researchers like Bessel van der Kolk have demonstrated that trauma is stored not just in the mind but deeply within the body’s nervous system. This means that traditional talk therapy alone may not be enough to resolve the underlying physiological patterns that keep you on edge or in survival mode.

Our autonomic nervous system (ANS) governs our reactions to threat and safety, fluctuating between sympathetic (fight/flight), parasympathetic (rest/digest), and the more recently understood social engagement system described by Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory. Healing requires not just intellectual insight but a recalibration of these physiological states.

What Is Mindful Resting?

Mindful resting is an intentional practice of doing nothing while staying fully present in your body and breath. Unlike passive rest or distraction, mindful resting engages your parasympathetic nervous system, helping you downshift from hyperarousal or dissociation into a state of safety and regeneration.

Deb Dana, a leading expert on Polyvagal Theory, emphasizes that nervous system regulation is less about forcing relaxation and more about creating conditions where your nervous system feels safe enough to rest. This means cultivating awareness without judgment—simply noticing sensations, thoughts, and feelings as they arise and settle.

The Science Behind Doing Nothing as Healing

Research into contemplative neuroscience and somatic psychology confirms that the brain and body need periods of rest to integrate experiences and stabilize neural pathways. Francine Shapiro’s work with EMDR also highlights the importance of bilateral stimulation and rest phases for trauma processing.

Physiologically, when you engage in mindful resting, vagal tone increases. Vagal tone—the activity of the vagus nerve—is a key biomarker of nervous system health that predicts emotional regulation, social engagement, and resilience. Higher vagal tone means your body can better shift between states rather than get stuck in fight, flight, or freeze.

What Happens in Your Body During Mindful Resting?

  • Heart rate variability (HRV) improves: Indicating better autonomic flexibility.
  • Breathing slows and deepens: Stimulating the parasympathetic system.
  • Muscle tension decreases: Allowing the body to release stored trauma.
  • Brain activity shifts: From stress-related regions to those supporting calm and integration.

Why Is It So Hard to Do Nothing?

Many people have an internalized belief that rest is lazy or unproductive. Gabor Maté points out that in trauma, hypervigilance becomes a default survival strategy. The nervous system is on constant alert, making true rest feel unsafe or impossible.

Additionally, cultural conditioning often values busyness and distraction as signs of worth and identity. This leads to chronic activation of the sympathetic nervous system and exhaustion.

“Healing is not about doing more; sometimes it’s about allowing yourself to be, to rest, and to feel safe in your own body.” — The Oracle Lover

Mindful Resting Practices to Recalibrate Your Nervous System

Here are effective ways to cultivate mindful rest that supports nervous system healing:

  • Body Scan Meditation: Slow, nonjudgmental attention to bodily sensations from head to toe helps anchor awareness and release tension.
  • Breath Awareness: Focus on the rhythm of your breath, noticing its natural flow without trying to control it. This stimulates parasympathetic activation.
  • Supported Resting Posture: Lie down or sit comfortably with props to fully support your body, allowing muscles to let go.
  • Nature Immersion: Mindfully resting in natural settings enhances vagal tone and feelings of safety through sensory input.
  • Somatic Tracking: Following subtle shifts in sensation helps the nervous system process and discharge stuck energy (a technique advocated by Peter Levine).

Integrating Mindful Rest in Your Healing Journey

Mindful resting is not a one-off fix but a skill to cultivate over time. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy underlines the importance of consistent somatic practices to rewrite trauma patterns. Start with a few minutes daily and gradually extend as your system tolerates.

Notice any resistance—this is expected as your nervous system tests safety boundaries. Approach these moments with curiosity, not judgment. It’s often helpful to combine mindful resting with other somatic tools like grounding exercises or safe movement.

When to Seek Support

Some trauma survivors may find resting challenging or triggering, especially if freeze or dissociation patterns arise. Dan Siegel’s work on interpersonal neurobiology stresses the value of connection in healing—consider working with a trauma-informed somatic therapist if you need support navigating these responses.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model also highlights how parts of us may resist rest out of protective intent. Therapies that address these internal conflicts can make mindful resting safer and more accessible.

Conclusion: The Art of Doing Nothing as a Radical Act of Healing

In a world that glorifies doing, mindful resting is a radical, science-backed act of self-care. It helps regulate your nervous system, integrate trauma, and reclaim your innate capacity to feel safe and whole.

By learning to do nothing with intention and presence, you tune into your body’s wisdom and activate healing pathways often missed by talk therapy alone. Remember, healing is a process of becoming—not a project to fix.

faqs.q: Why is mindful resting important for trauma healing?
faqs.a: Mindful resting helps regulate the nervous system by activating the parasympathetic state, which supports safety and integration of traumatic experiences.

faqs.q: How does mindful resting differ from regular rest or sleep?
faqs.a: Mindful resting involves conscious awareness and presence during rest, whereas regular rest or sleep may not engage nervous system regulation actively or with intention.

faqs.q: Can mindful resting be challenging for people with trauma histories?
faqs.a: Yes, because trauma can make the nervous system hypervigilant or prone to dissociation, making rest feel unsafe. Supportive practices and professional guidance can help.

faqs.q: How often should I practice mindful resting to see benefits?
faqs.a: Starting with a few minutes daily and gradually increasing as tolerated is effective. Consistency is key to retraining the nervous system over time.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: This book offers practical guidance on using Polyvagal Theory to understand and regulate the nervous system, essential for mindful resting practices.

metaDescription: Learn how mindful resting heals your nervous system and why doing nothing is a powerful self-care tool for trauma recovery.
author: The Oracle Lover
